ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn term for Earl Grey tea; closest parent is black tea beverage (FOODON_03301103). Earl Grey is a bergamot-flavoured black tea.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Tea, black) is the closest parent; no specific Earl Grey sub-code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34728003 (Tea substance) is the closest available term; no Earl Grey-specific SNOMED CT code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed Earl Grey tea, approximately 240 ml, served hot or iced, typically without milk or sugar unless specified.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot, leaf, black — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Earl Grey is a bergamot-flavoured black tea. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea (FDC ID 14355). Bergamot oil is present in trace quantities and does not materially alter the macronutrient profile of the brewed beverage. Values represent plain brewed Earl Grey without milk or sugar, as served in a standard café preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages — plain brewed black tea contains negligible fermentable carbohydrate; GI conventionally assigned as effectively zero
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea, including Earl Grey, contains approximately 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 ml and no glycemic carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a specific GI value for Earl Grey tea. By analogy with plain brewed black tea and other non-caloric beverages, GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) per Project Veritas convention for zero-glycemic beverages. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 0.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 ml); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain Earl Grey tea is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content is negligible at approximately 7 mg per 240 ml serving, and regular black tea consumption has been associated with modest reductions in systolic blood pressure in some observational studies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and effectively zero glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain Earl Grey tea without added sugar or milk is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; it contributes no glycemic carbohydrates, and polyphenols in black tea may support insulin sensitivity, though individuals should avoid sweetened or condensed-milk preparations.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of flavonoid antioxidants from black tea
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Earl Grey tea is a heart-friendly beverage when consumed plain; it contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, and the flavonoids in black tea, particularly theaflavins and thearubigins, have been associated with improved endothelial function and reduced LDL oxidation in clinical studies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Earl Grey tea is a classic flavoured black tea blend distinguished by the addition of bergamot oil, extracted from the rind of the Citrus bergamia fruit, which imparts a distinctive floral, citrus aroma and a slightly perfumed flavour. The tea is brewed by steeping black tea leaves — typically a blend of Chinese or Indian origin — in hot water for three to five minutes. The resulting beverage is light amber to dark brown in colour, with a smooth, mildly astringent taste and a pronounced bergamot fragrance. Earl Grey is served hot or iced, and may be consumed plain or with milk, lemon, or sweetener according to preference. In Singapore, it is commonly offered at cafés and kopitiam-style establishments as a premium tea option, often priced at SGD 5–7 per cup. Nutritionally, plain brewed Earl Grey tea is essentially calorie-free, containing approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml, with negligible protein, fat, and carbohydrate. It provides trace amounts of potassium, magnesium, and folate, and contains naturally occurring polyphenols including theaflavins, thearubigins, and catechins, which have antioxidant properties. The caffeine content of a standard 240 ml cup is approximately 40–70 mg, comparable to other black tea preparations. Earl Grey is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and individuals following halal, kosher, or gluten-free diets when consumed without dairy additions. It is clinically appropriate for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or full-fat milk.
common_user_queries: Is Earl Grey tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in Earl Grey tea?
Is Earl Grey tea suitable for hypertension?
What does Earl Grey taste like?
Does Earl Grey tea have caffeine?
What is bergamot in Earl Grey?

confidence_reason: Earl Grey tea is a well-characterised flavoured black tea. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy entry for plain brewed black tea (FDC ID 14355), which is an approved secondary global source. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for Earl Grey tea as a distinct item. The adaptation is defensible because bergamot oil is present in trace quantities and does not materially alter the macronutrient profile. GI is estimated by analogy as the University of Sydney GI Database does not list Earl Grey specifically. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Plain brewed Earl Grey tea without milk or sugar. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 14355 (brewed black tea). Bergamot oil contributes negligible macronutrients at typical infusion concentrations. protein_g is naturally zero as brewed black tea contains no measurable protein at standard infusion strength per USDA FDC ID 14355. fat_g is naturally zero as brewed black tea contains no fat per USDA FDC ID 14355. sugar_g is confirmed zero for plain unsweetened brewed black tea per USDA FDC ID 14355. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero for brewed black tea per USDA FDC ID 14355. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Project Veritas convention for zero-glycemic beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g per serving is negligible. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0 as confirmed by USDA FDC ID 14355 for brewed black tea. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as not present in brewed black tea per USDA FDC ID 14355. The Sinpopo Coffee – Funan listing on field survey data at SGD 6.00 is noted as the commercial context; this price reflects a café preparation which may include milk or flavouring additions not captured in the plain-tea nutrient profile. Ontology codes use closest_parent match types as no exact Earl Grey-specific codes exist in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT catalogues. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish requirements as this food is served in a Singapore café context.
